mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, make it this: Apple's trade secrets lawsuit against Open A I is the story of the week. A complete reversal of their partnership from two years ago, landing just as Open A I nears an I P O filing.

Story one. Apple versus Open A I gets uglier.

From your Axios A I plus and The Rundown. Apple sued Open A I on July tenth in federal court, alleging trade secret theft at every level. The filing claims hardware chief Tang Tan, a former Apple executive, had job candidates bring actual Apple parts to interviews for show and tell. Another engineer allegedly kept his Apple laptop and downloaded confidential files while working at Open A I. Open A I says it has no interest in other companies' trade secrets. Your A I Collective newsletter put it this way: everyone came for Sam Altman this week. Even Apple.

Why it matters: this is the sharpest breakup yet between big tech and the A I labs, and it targets Open A I's hardware ambitions right before its I P O.

Story two. The model race sprints on.

From your A I News by swyx: Open A I's Codex hit roughly seven million users, up more than ten times in six months, and is now adding about a million users a day. There's real debate over whether it has overtaken Claude Code. Open A I also shipped three new G P T five point six models, per your Axios: Sol as the flagship, Terra at half the cost, and Luna as the fast tier. Google delayed Gemini three point five Pro to July seventeenth for a rebuild. Meta announced Muse Spark one point one, with a million token context and its first paid developer A P I. And Anthropic extended access to its Claude Fable five model. From swyx's World's Fair essay: the field's new phase is building systems around agents, not just with them. Your Recode China A I adds that Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance and others are all shipping coding agents too.

Why it matters: the competition has moved from model quality to agents, hardware, and distribution. And China's labs are racing up the same curve.

Story three. Policy: watchdogs, job shocks, and disclosure.

From your Axios A I plus: DeepMind chief Demis Hassabis is urging a new A I watchdog to regulate powerful models before they pose serious risks. From your Rundown: more than two hundred economists and A I figures are pressing for action on labor disruption. M I T economist Daron Acemoglu estimates ten percent job displacement within a decade is likely. In Washington, the F T C opened public comment on an A I accuracy policy, and senators introduced a bill requiring disclosure of A I generated content. Also in your Reads: A I Friction Labs argues educators should stop drawing ethical lines in the sand about A I cheating, and rethink assessment instead.

Why it matters: regulation talk is shifting from hypothetical risk to concrete rules. Accuracy, labeling, and jobs. Exactly the terrain comms teams will have to navigate.

Story four. Chips: the H two hundred experiment.

The U S is now months into conditional Nvidia H two hundred exports to China. Capped at half of U S volumes, independently tested, with a twenty five percent tariff. Chinese firms have ordered over two million units for this year. Commerce reaffirmed the rules follow Chinese companies' subsidiaries abroad. Meanwhile, Huawei's Ascend chips keep gaining ground at home, and T S M C posted record revenue. Tech Policy Press notes the old bipartisan consensus on export controls has fractured into a standoff between Congress, the White House, and industry.

Why it matters: chip policy is now trade policy, industrial policy, and A I policy all at once. And it's wobbling.

Story five. Media watch.

From your Status newsletter: C N N boss Mark Thompson quietly visited the White House last Thursday. Other editions cover California's attorney general taking aim at Bari Weiss, Larry Ellison's California nightmare, and a traffic meltdown across MAGA media. From your Information: lessons from a year of T I T V, their daily tech video show.

Why it matters: you're in comms. The press, power, and politics triangle is shifting fast, and these are the people redrawing it.

Story six. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
